title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the compass. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Needle That Changed the World
content.text: Before the invention of the compass, navigation was a matter of guesswork, memory, and celestial observation. Sailors stayed close to the coastline, fearing that once land dipped below the horizon, they would be lost to the vast, featureless expanse of the ocean. The ability to find North regardless of the weather or the time of day was the single most important breakthrough in maritime history. ||(41)|| [41]These early versions were not intended for navigation at all[/41]. Instead, they were used for geomancy and fortune-telling—the art of aligning buildings and graves with the invisible energy of the Earth. It took several centuries for scholars to realize that this 'magic' stone could be used to guide a ship across the sea. ||(42)|| [42]This transition to maritime use occurred during the Song Dynasty[/42]. By rubbing an iron needle against a piece of lodestone, sailors created a magnetized pointer that, when floated in a bowl of water, always turned toward the pole. This 'wet compass' allowed Chinese fleets to dominate the Indian Ocean long before European explorers reached its shores. ||(43)|| [43]The arrival of the technology in the West remains a subject of debate among historians[/43]. Some argue it traveled along the Silk Road, while others believe it was independently discovered by Mediterranean sailors. Regardless of its origin, the European 'dry compass'—which mounted the needle on a pivoting pin—proved to be more stable on the rolling deck of a ship. ||(44)|| [44]With this new confidence, the era of 'coastal hugging' ended[/44]. Navigators like Christopher Columbus and Ferdinand Magellan could now strike out into the open Atlantic and Pacific, knowing they could maintain a consistent heading even during weeks of heavy fog or cloudy nights. The world was suddenly open to exploration and, eventually, colonization. ||(45)|| [45]However, as voyages grew longer, a new problem emerged[/45]. Navigators noticed that the compass needle did not always point to 'True North'—the geographical North Pole. Instead, it pointed toward the 'Magnetic North Pole,' and the gap between the two, known as magnetic declination, varied depending on where you were on the globe. ||(46)|| [46]Today, while we rely on GPS satellites and digital sensors, the humble magnetic needle is still found in every emergency kit[/46]. It requires no batteries, no signal, and no software. It is a direct, physical link to the magnetic heart of our planet, a tool that remains as reliable today as it was a thousand years ago.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the first recorded use of magnetism [/41] for direction-finding appears in Chinese texts from the Han Dynasty. These documents describe a 'South-pointing spoon' made of lodestone, a naturally magnetized iron ore, which rotated on a smooth bronze plate.
paragraphs.B: However, [42] this spiritual application [/42] eventually gave way to practical necessity. As trade routes expanded further into the open sea, the need for a reliable directional tool outweighed the mystical traditions, and the lodestone was adapted for the first primitive navigational devices.
paragraphs.C: This [43] technological advantage [/43] gave China a brief period of naval supremacy. Their massive 'treasure ships' were able to navigate with a precision that was unmatched by any other civilization at the time, establishing trade links as far as the coast of East Africa.
paragraphs.D: The [44] introduction of the pivot [/44] was a crucial refinement. By eliminating the need for a bowl of water, which was prone to spilling in rough weather, the compass became a rugged, reliable instrument that could be housed in a protective box called a binnacle.
paragraphs.E: This [45] sudden expansion of horizons [/45] led to the creation of more accurate charts known as portolan maps. These maps were crisscrossed with 'rhumb lines' that corresponded to compass bearings, allowing sailors to plot a direct course between two distant ports with mathematical certainty.
paragraphs.F: Solving [46] this navigational discrepancy [/46] required centuries of observation and the creation of complex tables. It led to the discovery that the Earth’s magnetic field is not static, but constantly shifting, a phenomenon that scientists are still studying today using advanced satellite data.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern sailors still prefer [/47] the traditional look of a brass compass over a digital screen. They argue that the physical movement of the needle provides a more intuitive sense of direction than a fluctuating number on a display, especially in high-stress situations.

questions.explanation: A introduces the Han Dynasty and 'lodestone', which explains the 'early versions' used for geomancy mentioned after the gap.
B refers to the 'spiritual application' (geomancy/fortune-telling) and explains the transition to 'practical necessity' (navigation) described in the text.
C refers to the 'technological advantage' of the wet compass and links to the Chinese 'treasure ships' and their naval dominance.
D explains the 'introduction of the pivot', which is the defining feature of the 'dry compass' mentioned before the gap.
E links the 'expansion of horizons' to the end of 'coastal hugging' and the development of 'portolan maps' mentioned in the surrounding text.
F refers to 'this navigational discrepancy' (magnetic declination), explaining the scientific efforts to solve the problem mentioned before the gap.
